meta.purpose: Define reasoning chains behind management principles — why upstream diagnosis matters more than downstream intervention.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why a management principle exists or seeks deeper rationale.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why diagnosing upstream system conditions is the single most important management skill.
logic: A downstream intervention (coaching the person, giving a warning, setting a tighter deadline) treats the symptom — the person's behavior.
If the system condition that produced the behavior is unchanged, the next person in that role will exhibit the same behavior.
The manager spends a career replacing people while the system reliably breaks each replacement.
This is the most expensive management failure mode — infinite churn without performance improvement.
Therefore, before any individual intervention, always ask: 'Would a different person in this same system produce the same result?' If yes, fix the system first.
reasoning_chains.so_what: The first question after any execution failure is never 'what is wrong with this person' but 'what in the system made this outcome rational.' Answer that before deciding on action.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why providing clarity produces more execution than applying pressure.
logic: Urgency (deadlines, pressure, escalation) increases speed — the rate at which work is attempted.
Clarity (standards, priorities, definitions of done) increases direction — the accuracy with which work is aimed.
Speed without direction produces busy teams that deliver the wrong things quickly.
Direction without speed may be slower but at least produces correct outcomes.
In most organizations, the bottleneck is not speed. It is confusion about what to do and what good looks like.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before you tighten the deadline, tighten the standard. If the team cannot self-assess whether their work is correct, the deadline does not matter.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why teams that hide problems eventually fail, regardless of talent.
logic: Every team generates problems. The difference between high-performing and low-performing teams is not the number of problems — it is the speed of problem detection and resolution.
Problem detection speed depends on how safe team members feel surfacing bad news.
When surfacing problems is punished (blame, public shaming, career consequences), the team learns to hide problems.
Hidden problems grow. A problem found at week 1 costs 10x less than the same problem found at week 10.
Therefore, psychological safety is not a 'nice to have' cultural value. It is performance infrastructure.
reasoning_chains.so_what: The manager's response to the first early-surfaced problem of the quarter determines whether problems will be surfaced or hidden for the rest of the quarter. Make that response count.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Why the best-managed teams need the least management.
logic: The primary function of a manager is to make decisions the team cannot make for themselves: what to do, how well, by when.
If these decisions are encoded as clear standards, the team can self-manage the execution.
The manager is freed from daily decision-making and can focus on higher-leverage work: strategy, system design, capability building.
A team that needs the manager present to make daily decisions has a manager bottleneck and an uncalibrated team.
Therefore, the manager's goal should be to make themselves progressively unnecessary for daily operations.
reasoning_chains.so_what: After every decision, ask: 'Could I turn this into a standard so I don't have to make this decision again?' Invest in standard creation, not in decision repetition.
